---
title: "Ch 5 — Squares and Square Roots"
subtitle: "Tile a square — count its sides"
lesson-id: "cbse8.squares-and-square-roots"
tier: "cbse8"
ncert-class: 8
ncert-chapter: 5
ncert-chapter-title: "Squares and Square Roots"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["t1.multiply-as-area", "t1.divisibility-primes"]
unlocks: ["cbse8.cubes-and-cube-roots", "cbse9.number-systems", "cbse10.real-numbers"]
applied-domains: ["research", "encryption"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you compute $7 \times 7$ in your head? If not, revisit
   **[Multiplication as Area](../../tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)**.
2. Do you know what a *prime number* is, and can you list the primes up
   to $20$? If not, revisit
   **[Divisibility and Primes](../../tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)**.
3. Can you write $36$ as a product of primes?
4. Have you ever drawn a square grid?

If three were a confident yes, read on.
:::

## Touch

Take a sheet of squared graph paper, or imagine one. Colour in a $1
\times 1$ square. Now a $2 \times 2$. Now a $3 \times 3$. Count the
unit squares in each.

If no graph paper, the slider below builds them for you.

```{ojs}
viewof n = Inputs.range([1, 12], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "Side length n"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const cells = [];
  const sz = 22;
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++)
    for (let j = 0; j < n; j++)
      cells.push(`<rect x="${j*sz}" y="${i*sz}" width="${sz-2}" height="${sz-2}" fill="#bde0fe" stroke="#1d4ed8"/>`);
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;">
    <svg width="${n*sz+2}" height="${n*sz+2}">${cells.join("")}</svg>
    <div style="margin-top:0.4rem;color:#444;">
      side length <b>${n}</b> &nbsp; - &nbsp; total cells <b>${n*n}</b>
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Slide $n$ from $1$ up to $12$. Watch the cell count: $1, 4, 9, 16, 25,
36, \ldots$ Now play the game in reverse: if I tell you a square has
$49$ cells, what was its side?

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. The numbers $1, 4, 9, 16, 25, \ldots$ have a pattern. Can you
   describe it without using the word "square"?
2. Look at consecutive differences: $4-1 = 3$, $9-4 = 5$, $16-9 = 7$,
   $25-16 = 9$. What sequence is that?
3. What kind of number could the side of a $50$-cell square be?
4. Could you tile a square with exactly $1{,}000$ unit cells?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice (don't peek until you've thought)

The differences $3, 5, 7, 9$ are the consecutive odd numbers. So
$1 + 3 + 5 + 7 = 16 = 4^2$. The first $n$ odd numbers always sum to
$n^2$ — a beautiful fact you can prove with pictures.

A $1{,}000$-cell square cannot exist with a whole-number side, because
$\sqrt{1000}$ is not a whole number. Some numbers are *perfect
squares*; most are not.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Recognise perfect squares

Which of these are perfect squares (the area of a square with
whole-number side)? $25, \ 30, \ 49, \ 50, \ 64, \ 100$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Try to find a whole number $n$ with $n^2$ equal to each.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$25 = 5^2$, $49 = 7^2$, $64 = 8^2$, $100 = 10^2$ — perfect squares.
$30$ and $50$ are not (no whole $n$ with $n^2 = 30$ or $50$).
</details>

### Problem 2 — Square via prime factors

Use prime factorisation to decide whether $144$ is a perfect square.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Factor $144$ all the way to primes.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$144 = 2^4 \times 3^2$. For the number to be a perfect square, every
prime power must be even. Are they?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$144 = 2^4 \cdot 3^2$. Both exponents are even. So $144 = (2^2 \cdot 3)^2
= 12^2$ — yes, a perfect square. $\sqrt{144} = 12$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — A non-square

Is $72$ a perfect square? If not, what is the smallest whole number you
can multiply $72$ by to get a perfect square?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Factor $72$. Look for any prime that appears an *odd* number of times.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$72 = 2^3 \cdot 3^2$. The $2$'s exponent is odd; multiply by one more
$2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$72 \times 2 = 144 = 12^2$. Smallest multiplier is $\boxed{2}$, giving
$144$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Long division for $\sqrt{1369}$

Use the long-division method (or any method you know) to find
$\sqrt{1369}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pair the digits from the right: $\overline{13}\,\overline{69}$. Find the
largest integer $a$ with $a^2 \leq 13$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
$3^2 = 9 \leq 13$. Bring down $69$. Form $469$. Find the largest digit
$b$ with $(60 + b) \cdot b \leq 469$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- Pair: $\overline{13}\,\overline{69}$.
- $3^2 = 9$; remainder $13 - 9 = 4$. Bring down $69 \to 469$.
- Double the answer-so-far: $6$. Try $b$: $67 \times 7 = 469$. Match.
- $\sqrt{1369} = 37$.

Verify: $37^2 = 1369$. ✓
</details>

### Problem 5 — A non-perfect square

Estimate $\sqrt{50}$ to one decimal place, without a calculator.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$7^2 = 49$ and $8^2 = 64$. So $\sqrt{50}$ is between $7$ and $8$, much
closer to $7$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7^2 = 49$, just $1$ short of $50$. Try $7.1$: $7.1^2 = 50.41$. So
$\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$. To one decimal: $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.1$.

(Many numbers do not have a tidy square-root expression — they are
*irrational*. We meet that idea fully in Class 9.)
</details>

### Problem 6 — Sum of odd numbers

Without computing, write down $1 + 3 + 5 + 7 + 9 + 11 + 13$ as a square.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Count the terms.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Seven consecutive odd numbers, so the sum is $7^2 = 49$.
</details>

You have just used four tools — visual tile counting, prime
factorisation, long division, and the sum-of-odd-numbers identity — to
work with squares.

::: {.fun-fact}
Around 1800 BCE, Babylonian scribes pressed a clay tablet now catalogued
as **YBC 7289**, which sits in the Yale Babylonian Collection. On it,
they wrote $\sqrt{2}$ in base-60 as $1; 24, 51, 10$ — accurate to about
**six decimal places** ($\approx 1.41421296$). Almost four thousand
years before the long-division algorithm you just used, an unnamed
clerk had already extracted square roots well enough to lay out
diagonals of a square room — and the tablet's value beats most modern
schoolchildren's first attempt.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A perfect square is a number whose **prime factors come in matched
pairs**. That single picture explains everything: prime-factor pairing
gives the test, splits each pair into one factor of the root, tells you
the smallest multiplier to fix a non-square, and connects squares to
the geometry of an $n \times n$ tile. Once you see "pairs of primes",
the chapter collapses into one idea.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **perfect square** is a whole number of the form $n^2$ where $n$ is a
whole number.

The **square root** of $a \geq 0$ is the non-negative number $b$ with
$b^2 = a$, written $\sqrt{a}$.

**Test by primes:** a positive integer is a perfect square if and only
if every prime in its factorisation appears an *even* number of times.

**Sum-of-odds identity:** $1 + 3 + 5 + \cdots + (2n-1) = n^2$.

:::

A short verse, in the spirit of Bhāskara's *Līlāvatī*:

> *Even powers in primes — a perfect square stands;*
> *Odd one peeking — the form falls short.*
> *Pair the digits from right, division step descends —*
> *The root emerges as the column count.*

Two extra patterns worth remembering:

- **Last-digit shortcut.** A perfect square ends in $0, 1, 4, 5, 6,$
  or $9$. So $58, 73, 92, 100002$ — three of them are *not* perfect
  squares immediately by last digit.
- **Pythagorean triples.** $3^2 + 4^2 = 5^2$, $5^2 + 12^2 = 13^2$,
  $8^2 + 15^2 = 17^2$. These pop up everywhere from tile-laying to
  encryption.

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find the smallest whole number by which $1{,}008$ must be divided so
> that the quotient is a perfect square. Then find the square root of
> the quotient.

Competent solver's path:

1. Prime-factorise: $1008 = 2^4 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 7$.
2. The odd one out is $7$ (appears once). Divide by $7$:
   $1008 / 7 = 144 = 2^4 \cdot 3^2$.
3. $\sqrt{144} = 12$.

**Answer: divide by 7; root of quotient is 12.**

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Compute $\sqrt{225}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$15^2 = 225$. So $\sqrt{225} = 15$.
</details>

**W2.** Without computing, say which of these end in a digit that no
perfect square can end in: $48, \ 73, \ 100, \ 327, \ 81$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Squares end only in 0, 1, 4, 5, 6, 9.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$48$ (ends in 8), $73$ (ends in 3), $327$ (ends in 7) — none of these
can be perfect squares.
</details>

**W3.** Write the prime factorisation of $400$ and use it to compute
$\sqrt{400}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$400 = 2^4 \cdot 5^2$. So $\sqrt{400} = 2^2 \cdot 5 = 20$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Find the smallest number to multiply $252$ by so the result is a
perfect square.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Factor $252 = 2^2 \cdot 3^2 \cdot 7$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The exponent of $7$ is odd. Multiply by $7$: $252 \times 7 = 1764 =
42^2$. Smallest multiplier: **7**.
</details>

**P2.** Use the long-division method to find $\sqrt{2025}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Pair: $\overline{20}\,\overline{25}$. Largest $a$ with $a^2 \leq 20$ is
$4$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $4^2 = 16$. Remainder $4$. Bring $25 \to 425$.
- Double $4 \to 8$. Try $b$: $85 \times 5 = 425$. Match.
- $\sqrt{2025} = 45$.
</details>

**P3.** A square hall has area $784\,\text{m}^2$. What is its side
length?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Side $= \sqrt{\text{area}}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{784} = 28$ m. (Check by $784 = 2^4 \cdot 7^2$, so root $= 4 \cdot 7 = 28$.)
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Find the smallest whole $k$ such that $1000 + k$ is a perfect
square.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$31^2 = 961$, $32^2 = 1024$. Which is the next perfect square after
$1000$?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$32^2 = 1024$. So $k = 24$.
</details>

**S2.** A gardener has $2401$ saplings. He wants to arrange them in a
perfect square pattern (rows = columns). How many in each row, and how
many rows? Solve using prime factorisation.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sqrt{2401} = ?$. Factor $2401$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2401 = 7^4$. So $\sqrt{2401} = 7^2 = 49$. Arrangement: $\boxed{49
\times 49}$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** Which of these is *not* a perfect square?
(a) $169$ (b) $196$ (c) $225$ (d) $200$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$169 = 13^2$, $196 = 14^2$, $225 = 15^2$. $200$ is between $14^2 = 196$
and $15^2 = 225$ — not a perfect square. **Answer: (d).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find the square root of $7744$ by prime
factorisation.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$7744 / 2 = 3872 / 2 = 1936$. Continue.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$7744 = 2^6 \cdot 11^2$. $\sqrt{7744} = 2^3 \cdot 11 = 88$.

Check: $88 \times 88 = 88 \times 88 = 7744$. ✓
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Find the smallest 4-digit perfect square.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
4-digit numbers start at 1000. We need the smallest $n^2 \geq 1000$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.6$. So $n = 32$ is the smallest integer with
$n^2 \geq 1000$. $32^2 = 1024$.

**Smallest 4-digit perfect square: $1024$.**
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** (i) Find the least number that must be added
to $5607$ to make it a perfect square. (ii) Hence find the square root
of the resulting number. (iii) Find the least number that must be
*subtracted* from $5607$ to make it a perfect square, and the square
root of that number.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$74^2 = 5476$, $75^2 = 5625$, $76^2 = 5776$. Locate $5607$ between
consecutive squares.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
For (i)/(ii) use the *next* perfect square; for (iii) use the *previous*.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$74^2 = 5476$, $75^2 = 5625$. So $5607$ sits between $5476$ and $5625$.

**(i)** Add: $5625 - 5607 = 18$.

**(ii)** $\sqrt{5625} = 75$.

**(iii)** Subtract: $5607 - 5476 = 131$. $\sqrt{5476} = 74$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Problem 5.7 (least number to add
or subtract for a perfect square) is a near-twin of the 5-mark item.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2020 Set 1, Q14 ("smallest multiplier
to make a perfect square") matches *Working P1* exactly.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\sqrt{16} = \pm 4$."** By convention $\sqrt{16}$ denotes only
   the non-negative root, $4$. Both $4$ and $-4$ square to $16$, but
   the symbol $\sqrt{\cdot}$ picks the non-negative one.
2. **"Every number has a tidy square root."** No. Most do not.
   $\sqrt{2}, \sqrt{3}, \sqrt{50}$ are *irrational* — they cannot be
   written as $\tfrac{p}{q}$. We will see this rigorously in Class 9.
3. **"To check if $n$ is a perfect square, just look at its last
   digit."** The last-digit test only *rules out* — a number ending in
   $1$ might or might not be a perfect square (e.g., $21$ isn't).
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [perfect square](/glossary.qmd#perfect-square) is $n^2$ for some
   whole $n$ — and the [square root](/glossary.qmd#square-root)
   $\sqrt{a}$ is the non-negative number that squares to $a$.**
2. **The [prime factorisation](/glossary.qmd#prime-factorisation) test:
   a positive integer is a perfect square iff every
   [prime](/glossary.qmd#prime) appears an even number of times. Half
   the exponents and you have the root.**
3. **Most numbers are *not* perfect squares — their roots are
   [irrational](/glossary.qmd#irrational-number). Use long division for
   a numerical estimate; use the sum-of-odds identity
   $1 + 3 + 5 + \cdots + (2n-1) = n^2$ for instant pattern recognition.**

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you explain to a friend, without notes, *why* the prime
   factor test works?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   *Practice* problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Given a five-digit number, can you find its square root by long
   division without panicking?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Cubes and Cube Roots](../06-cubes-and-cube-roots/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo *Working P2* and the 5-mark board item, then move on.
- **0–1 yes:** rebuild from *Discover Problem 4*. Long division is the
  technique most learners stumble on first.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Multiplication as Area](../../tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)** — visualising $n^2$ as the area of an $n \times n$ square.
- **[Divisibility and Primes](../../tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)** — prime factorisation as the test.

### What this unlocks

- **[Cubes and Cube Roots](../06-cubes-and-cube-roots/index.qmd)** — same idea, one dimension up.
- **[Class 9 — Number Systems](../../tier-cbse-09-class-9/01-number-systems/index.qmd)** — irrationals enter formally; $\sqrt{2}$ is shown to be irrational.
- **[Class 10 — Real Numbers](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/01-real-numbers/index.qmd)** — fundamental theorem of arithmetic, formalising the prime-factor test.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 8, Chapter 5 — Squares and Square Roots** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised syllabus). Covers Sections 5.1–5.4: properties of
  squares, square roots by prime factorisation and long division, and
  Pythagorean triples.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different pedagogical angle):
  [`t2.square-roots-irrationals`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd)
  goes deeper into irrationality; this chapter focuses on computational
  technique.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**, which remains the canonical
  source for the board paper.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The Pythagorean theorem ($a^2 + b^2 = c^2$) is the seed
of every distance computation in mathematics. Once you trust square
roots, you have unlocked Euclidean geometry, complex numbers, and
quantum mechanics.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Encryption.** RSA encryption rests on the difficulty of factoring a
large integer that is a product of two primes — essentially the
*reverse* of the prime-factor test you just used to compute square
roots. The asymmetry between multiplication (easy) and factoring (hard)
is the whole reason internet payments work.
:::
